Good evening everybody. Dan Kennedy. I'm here with Susan Adams to appease my paranoia. Susan, are you there? Yes, I'm here Dan. Wonderful. Ain't it great when a plan comes together and technology works. It doesn't happen every day, does it? That's absolutely right. Well folks, thanks for joining us tonight. You're in for a great call, a timely call, an important call. Susan, as you undoubtedly know, because you are here, has spent a fair amount of time very successfully selling to high net worth individuals, high income individuals. And so that is why we have gathered here, is to pick her brain. But Susan, maybe for those who are brand new and therefore will have missed the two installment interview with you in the most recent issues of the NoBS marketing to the Fluent All Letter, or haven't read your biography. Maybe you can give everybody the brief thumbnail of who you are and how you've arrived here. Well sure, I'd be happy to. I'll give you the CliffsNotes version. I started out in sales a few years ago, I'll say. My first job was actually selling encyclopedias. One of those jobs where you kind of met with people every night and had a pre-rehearsed script and no business cards. About 15 years later, I found myself out on a veranda at Pebble Beach having a glass of wine with a couple of guys who probably had a combined net worth of about $200 million. In the interim, I had worked for big companies like General Electric and Pitney Bows and done everything from selling encyclopedias to cold calling to finally selling to millionaires. And mostly in the selling to millionaires category, you've been dealing with fractional jet ownership? Yes. I noticed the other day, I'm sure you noticed that recession be damned, Netschett's just ordered $1.9 billion worth of airplanes. Yes. The one category that is still growing, and I have a lot of friends that actually sell the whole aircraft, the whole Gulf Streams that sell for $30, $40, $50 million, those aircraft are all on three and four year backlogs because there's such huge, and it's global demand. There's demand coming from Russia, from the Middle East, and there's always been strong demand from North America. The group that still has considerable amounts of money is definitely the affluent and the super affluent. Yes, Trump just sold, I don't know if you saw that news today. For $95 million. Yes, the house, the property that he sold, if people missed it in the news today, he sold this massive estate that he paid $41 million. Yes, but he paid $41 million, he just sold for $95 million four years later to a Russian, by the way, who's on the Forbes 400 list. But I was on a program with Trump right after he bought that property, and he talked about how it essentially was a foreclosure. That $41 million? Yes. He joked that it was a fixer-upper, and he bought it from a bankrupt billionaire. It was a great story, and at the time people were, of course, believing he could grossly overpaid and all of that, and obviously there's somebody out there. And global versus domestic is, if we have time, sort of the whole no boundaries question I do want to ultimately get to tonight. But for starters, I think what will interest people, I'm sure that sitting on the veranda selling to the multi-millionaires is a lot more fun than being on the living room floor with the smelly dog and the two kids and the guy who can't spell a world book, making the encyclopedia presentation. Yes. Having done that type of selling myself as well, I do understand. I do understand that the veranda scene is a more pleasant scene. Most people will think, some people, first of all, will think it's the entire market, the entire opportunity to do business with this level of customer is beyond their reach or inappropriate for their business, and most will think that selling to these people is dramatically, radically different. So maybe you could talk a little bit about what is different about selling to the affluent, the ultra affluent, the high net worth folks. Well, the thing that makes them different as customers is the thing that makes them the best customers, and that price is not an issue with them. There's no price discussion. It was the first job I ever held where you didn't have to spend a lot of the sales process defending your price. And the sales people that are on the call or people that are in businesses that are on the call tonight, how much of your time do you spend right now with your clients defending your price? Well, with this group, you don't have to defend price. And I would make the point, and I'll be honest to your comments on it, again, the leap to assumption is price doesn't matter solely because they're rich. But it's really my experience that, well, I mean, obviously that is true, but that's only part of this, that they have a unique psychology and philosophy about price themselves and a different set of priorities than price that it's important to understand. Right, price is wrapped up in value. It's all wrapped up in the value they perceive in your product or service. So price is a component, but it's a component as part of the value discussion. And all the research that's been done on this group of people, they all say, oh, well, the affluent buy on value. But then they never tell you, well, gee, as a business, how do you develop value for yourself or for your product or service with this group of people? And from my experience, value is created in a variety of ways. And a lot of it goes back to understanding your buyer as well. First, you have to know who you're selling against. You have to know your competition inside and out. You have to know their strengths and weaknesses. And you also have to have the ability to be able to change the conversation with this individual and change the buying criteria. And what I mean by this is, you know, when I tell people that I used to sell fractional ownership of jets, you know, the assumption is you were selling the jet. Yes, I was. I was selling the jet as a means of getting from point A to point B. But the jet wasn't what they were buying. The value of what they were buying came in the experience of owning the jet. And the experience of what buying the jet was going to be able to allow them to do. So through the course of the conversation, and, you know, you notice I always refer to sales as a conversation. That's what I did. I just had conversations with people all day long. Through the course of the conversation, you present the value of your product or service in the context of the experience that it will allow them to have. And once you have the ability to start doing that, you're communicating on a level with them that really resonates. And you're not talking about things that all your competitors are talking about, you know. For instance, if you, you know, were just showing an airplane, and I learned this because I would watch people as they did it, the people who, you know, were aircraft guys, which I was not an aircraft guy when I came into this business, they would start talking about, you know, the avionics and the engines. And I would literally watch, you know, a CEO's eyes just glaze over. He could have cared less about avionics and engines. All he wanted to do was get in the cabin and say, yep, this is a good size for me. I feel comfortable in the seat. The baggage will fit. Four sets of golf clubs. That was the extent of what he wanted to know about the airplane. Yeah. You know, and the process that you just described is a process that people will have to now go through with their own business to make this transition. To being effective with this market because like, like they were aviation guys, now everybody on the call is a blank guy, blank gal with the business they're already in. Yes. And so if there's a restaurant owner on the call, he's a restaurant guy at this point in the same way that, that those, that those folks were, were aviation guys. And so people are so accustomed to talking about the products, the services, the features, the benefits. They're explaining things more or less. That's right. That's right. And as they shift to these clients, they've got to make that change. I fly private myself. I don't own. I rent. But I had the same experience in, in initially finding the company I was going to do business with is people made the mistake of wanting to tell me, you know, about engine parts and RPM. The thrust per foot of the engine. Yeah. And, and I think it's important to understand different people are looking for different value, you know, they were also, I mean, like, for me, it is not about, although for many, I know it's a status by a reinforcement of self image. For me, it was totally about convenience and avoiding the hassle of the other means of getting there. And, and, and I think people like get locked into one way of presenting what it is and what they do as a one size fits all. Where, as you say, at some point a conversation when you're selling big ticket items to affluent people, a conversation is necessary to try and determine what the value is. So talk about ways that you get to understanding your buyer. Well, I think, you know, that again, and you can go back to, you know, there's, there's been a lot of research certainly done on the affluent. And, you know, something that I found very interesting that I don't see a lot of researchers talking about, they, they lump the affluent together in general terms, as you know, okay, where's this much money, this much money there in this demographic. And, and, you know, they'll come back with general themes about them. I actually kind of, I'm a big believer in market segmentation. And I think especially if you're going to be marketing to this particular group of people and selling to them, you can, you know, I'll use age as an example. You know, a, a guy in his 40s who, you know, is worth 20 or 30 million dollars is in a different mindset than a guy in his 60s or his 80s who is worth that same amount of money. And you can sell and market to people differently just based on what you know about them from their age group that they're in. Well, the, the married is very different than the single. Exactly. And, and I don't see anybody marketing in segmentation to, to the affluent. Everybody kind of, if you open the Wall Street Journal, which, you know, I read religiously pretty much every day, almost all the ads are exactly the same. They're all targeting the same kind of buyer. And the, and the ads are all very similar. And, you know, they, they'll talk about exclusivity and, you know, things like that. But nobody has ever, is really trying to reach beyond those general themes and dig a little bit deeper, you know, to find a message that really resonates because they understand the buyer. Even worse than the ads being the same and generic, the direct mail and the, and the material sent out to leads when they raise their hands is the same and generic. So, you know, I answer a lot of those ads as a student. And I'm amazed that there's no attempt to get even, even basic segmentation information like age, are you married or you single, you know, et cetera. Right, you have small children. Are you an empty nester, you know? Right. And the few times that they do get it, they don't use it because they're clearly sending me a generic package. Or if I'm on a list, I'm getting a generic direct mail piece. And I rail against this all the time. I think it's a combination of stupidity and laziness. And it is something that the small business person can do that big, if they will, that big companies are either ignorant of or too lazy or just too big and bureaucratic and unagile to do. And that is to segment customers and prospects and tweak messages so that you're really running five or six or seven marketing campaigns under one roof. Exactly. With new rants differences. Right, and the same goes back to even, you know, how you sell to them. You know, for instance, you know, a gentleman who was in his 70s or 80s, I always called them Mr. and Mrs. You know, we were never on a, it was always a very formal relationship. Whereas someone that was in their 40s, you know, they were in my age group and if I called them Mr. they'd say, oh, don't call me Mr. Call me Joe. You know, so even those little subtle things, you know, so if I was talking to someone who was, you know, in his 70s and called him Joe, that was a big faux pas. You know, so when, and I would say the majority of our owners, they were in their 60s more or less. That's where the bulk of them resided for this particular, I mean, and most of our owners had networks of over $20 million just to give everyone an idea of what market segmentation we're talking about. But I mean, I can tell you, I see that happening every day that no one is making any effort to say to, you know, someone in their 40s. For instance, for that person in their 40s, they're going to be much more in tune with the, you've made it message, you know, the, wow, you've arrived. Then someone in their 60s. Because by the time you're in your 60s, you've been there for a while. Right. Yeah. And in your 60s, you're thinking about different things. You're saying, yes, so what? I've arrived. But in your 40s, that is a message that appeals to you. And so, you know, as you, and this goes across all the demographics of the affluent as far as I'm concerned. So it can be the millionaire next door, you know, that you're marketing to in this way. But understanding just, you know, age differences can make a huge, you know, advantage for you and just how you market to these people. Well, and the brilliancy, one thing for people to catch here is to decide on the message that overlays what they sell to who they sell it to. Exactly. So you've described the message category, the you've made it message. And this is, you deserve it. And this is something you do now that you've made it. This is now you dine in a better restaurant. You, you, you're supposed to know about fine wines. You're supposed to buy better clothes, et cetera. And people have to sort of pick a message category and then pick a market category and then put their business into both of those things. What are some of the other mistakes? We've talked about a couple that you see people who are stepping up to marketing or selling to either millionaires or multi-millionaires. What are some of the other mistakes you see them most commonly making? I see, you know, especially, you know, and you mentioned this before, the feature benefit selling. The, you know, I mean, I do a lot of sales training now in the fractional market and the fractional real estate market, which is really, you know, very high end resorts. It's usually, you know, five star resorts and, you know, they're touring people through and talking about granite countertops. You know, and I'm sitting there saying, you know, if this person is, you know, affluent, they have granite countertops everywhere they go. So I don't, I don't think that's a compelling message that you're using in sales for them. So I think too many people get bogged down in the feature benefit selling. And I also think that not enough people use what I'll call story-based selling. Stories, you know, and, you know, and in copywriting, you know, which I know you've done extensively, stories are the most powerful way to get across any kind of message you're trying to get across to a client or a prospect. And it's much, you know, when people would ask me certain questions, you know, I mean, I used to get the, not the price is too high, but I always got the, how do people financially justify this? Because, you know, it doesn't financially make sense because it is a, I won't say a toy, but it is certainly an exclusive perk. And I would say to them, well, most of my owners have told me that they value the ability to get all their business done and still be at home at night to have dinner with their kids. And when I would say something like that, I never got that objection again because that was a message that resonated. It's really not the price. It's not feeling foolish about wasting money. Right? I mean, there are people often in buying premium priced goods or services, they really are looking for the justification that makes them a play. Exactly. And the ability to, again, you know, maybe visit three cities in a day and still be able to make their kids soccer game, making their kids soccer game was more valuable than all the business they did that day. So, the best clothing salesman I ever encountered. I went into a store in the form shops in Las Vegas, a Bernini store, and the guy sold me a suit for, you know, an unconscionable sum of money. I mean, that makes no sense. You know, it's not that much better. But, well, to make it short, the guy, I asked about price at least seven times and he never answered me. He moved me all the way through trying it on to getting it measured, to getting the tailor out there to when and where it was going to be delivered that evening where I was having dinner without answering the price question. And instead, he kept asking me questions that led to justifications. You know, what do you do for a living, right? And helping him, of course, there was someone along with me who was going the process. So, you know, he says, oh, he's a famous author and speaker. Well, this guy's off on a run now, right? Well, you've got to have the suit for all your speaking statements. The question for this suit is how good it looks under the lights and, you know, that someone like you in your position of importance, this is exactly the sort of, you know, and so every question led to an answer that came back to a justification for the price that, in fact, never got named. I mean, the guy was spectacular. He talked about everything but the price. Exactly. Exactly. And I was fine with it because he was elegant about it. You know, he was good about it. And he was good at telling stories. Well, and that's, you know, something, you know, as people who, you know, are on the call and listening tonight, you know, think about your own business and think about yourself and, you know, what is your story? You know, or a story about your product or service that would be compelling, you know, to an affluent individual. Because, again, most of these people, they're entrepreneurs. They're risk takers. They're people that started out as a small business owner and maybe built it into something that they sold for $50 million. So there's nothing that they appreciate more than, you know, a message from another entrepreneur. You know, so I would stop thinking, especially of the super affluent, as this group of, you know, behind the fence untouchables, you know, that you can't get to or that you have something that they don't want or they don't need. I certainly wouldn't view them that way at all. Well, speaking of behind the fence, let's switch gears just a bit. And rather than talking about selling to them, let's talk about the other thing that mystifies everybody, which is how to find them, how to connect with them in the first place so that you get the opportunity to sell to them. Well, I know in your book, which I thought, you know, for anyone who's on the call that hasn't read the Dan's affluent book, you really should, because I learned a ton in there. And, you know, there was stuff, you know, I had, you know, posted notes all through it, you know, with little notes on it that I made. So, you know, from a marketing perspective, you know, that's a great resource for anyone to say, hey, here's a great place to go to learn how to do direct mail and things like that. Some of the things that I talk about are some of the more non-traditional marketing things that I saw be very successful in my company. You know, for instance, again, you look at where these people go. Where do they hang out? Okay, well, they all hang out at pricey country clubs. And you say to yourself, well, I'm not going to go spend $200,000 or $300,000 on a country club membership. Well, you don't have to. The thing is, is that all those country clubs, they're always doing charity events. And most of those charity events, you can either buy a ticket and go to, or you can, you know, approach them once you know about the event and offer, you know, a product or service you have as a giveaway. At the charity event. And that gets you entrance into the event and it also gets you printed up into the program. You know, so that's one way, non-traditional way that I would say. Well, if I won't derail you to validate that, and I've been prospecting, which is the last thing at the moment that I want to do. But Carl and I, I don't know, now, within the past few weeks, we were members of the Cleveland Playhouse. And so we were at the opening night, pre-opening night thing before the public gets to go for the run of the Broadway cast of the Jersey Boys here. And so they have a big pre-opening night thing. There's a dinner and then there's the performance and then there's dancing and then there's a dessert reception and on and on and on. And so we are now doing one of my least favorite things in life to do, which is sitting at a table of 12 with, you know, 10 people I don't know. And in all likelihood, probably don't want to know, right? Right. And to no good purpose, going to have to deal with the question from civilians of what do you do, you know, to which there is no good answer. Right. But anyway, there we are, right? Now, had I been prospecting, it would have been a lay down. Exactly. Now, the age range of the couples at the table were my 50 ish range up to probably 80. The couple, one of the couples, the guy owns about a $10 million a year business here in Cleveland in a particular type of financial services where we have other members. I have a couple coaching members. I even have a client he would probably know, which I chose not to mention. But even with the sort of intended not to cause probing further answer to what I do, it immediately led to, you know, marketing questions. Right. You know, what do you know about this? And, you know, I've been thinking about doing this. What do you think about that? This thing gets picked up by two couples over and they own a chain of a particular type of store. I know one of their stores. It turns out that she has one of my books. Well, you know, I could have turned this into conversations leading to business without breaking a sweat. And I didn't and didn't want to. But these charity activities, I mean, when you are, get yourself involved on a committee or in a donor circle or and you pick something you like that you want to be involved with anyway, you are putting yourself in an ideal position to meet and multiply these kind of contacts. Right. And it's just more of a non-traditional way of marketing yourself. Well, yeah, it's non-marketing marketing, right? Now, you also, we talked about it a little bit in the interview in the newsletter. You were in our particularly adept at creating entree to this crowd through joint ventures with others who already have the entree. And our folks are big joint venture people. They understand it conceptually anyway. So if you would, maybe you can talk a little bit about that. Well, I mean, we use joint ventures all the time. And, you know, typically for us, you know, I mean, I'll use the Pebble Beach golf outing as a good example. We once a year would invite our owners to Pebble Beach. We'd pay for everything. We'd give them two first class tickets if they wanted to take their jet. They had to pay for it. So, but what we would do is to offset all the costs because it was really expensive. I think it came to a million dollars. We would invite other partners in and we would invite partners, you know, we would, I remember one dinner. We had cocktail hours sponsored by a company called Breitling and Breitling makes aviation watches. Well, these aviation watches sell for four to $5,000 a watch. During the time they were there, they sold 20 watches. So they more than made up whatever they were spending on the event. And now many people I'm sure are listening are saying, well, yeah, you had this huge marketing budget. You were doing jets. You know, you had this and that. What you have to do is take this down to your local market. I mean, for instance, I live in Pittsburgh, you know, Pittsburgh is not known as a big glam, you know, market, but there are plenty of things that go on in Pittsburgh that I could have tried to work from a joint venture perspective. You know, for instance, last year the US Open was here. Mario Lemieux has a golf tournament here every year. There's a vintage grand prix going on in the neighborhood around my house this coming weekend. So what I encourage people to do is, you know, look at your local market and look at complimentary joint venture partners, you know, that you can start to network with and through. And they're not competitors to you. They're people who have access to clients that you may want. And they're people that can actually, you know, then, you know, work in a group to either do an event, you know, you may get 10 to 15 different businesses together to all throw this big event and you all invite your customer lists. And guess what? Now you've got a guest list of three to 400 affluent people all in the same room, exposing them to your product or service. So the interesting thing that you'll find is as you start planning these events or going to these events, we always found, and again, you know, we were servicing a very high net worth individual. They wanted to come because it was such a great opportunity for them to network with other business owners. And they were always looking for joint ventures. So, you know, what you'll find is, you know, a lot of times people will even come attend because they everybody wants to be in a room with people like themselves. And that's certainly true with the affluent. That's why they join country clubs. That's why they go to five star restaurants so they can be surrounded by people like themselves. People are looking for a writer down or there was one for you. Everybody wants to be in a room with people like themselves. That's a good one. Right, it's just validation. I would imagine and I know that, you know, my book's out very recently, very new. And one of the skeptical reactions, I guess, from people, and you and I were talking about it really before we started the call, is that, oh, you know, for heaven's sakes, we're in a recession and the stock market's in the dumper. And, you know, nobody's really buying anything. And certainly this is not the time to be trying to upscale my business and start marketing to a higher level of customer than I've been marketing to before. In fact, better I should go hide in a cave and wait until it's all over. And we talked about it a little bit before the call. I mean, you agree, I'm sure, that if anything now, I mean, it would be smart for any business to be doing this at any time. But actually the timing is superb rather than wrong. Well, I mean, I'm not an economist, but I certainly don't see any indication on the horizon that things are going to get dramatically better for the middle class, you know, in the foreseeable future. And, you know, I mean, I study the affluent. That's my business. I, you know, read reports every day from, you know, luxury markets and real estate markets and financial markets, all on this group of people. And from the millionaire next door, which is, you know, someone with a net worth of a million dollars or more, you know, obviously to the, you know, billionaires, they are still spending. I mean, they may have pulled back, you know, in certain areas, but they're still spending. You know, I read something the other day where hot, you know, jewelry is still flying off the shelves. And, you know, another report I read where 42% of high net worth individuals say this is a great time to buy a second home because the values of the houses are down. The thing about this group of people is they're very in tune with financial things. And they know if the economy is in a recession, it's a great opportunity for them because they do have money and they will spend. And I know when you did your call for your book last week, there was a gentleman who I think he did, he was the swimming pools. And he was, you know, asking questions about, you know, how is this a good time, you know, to be getting into that market. Absolutely, because the wealthy are still buying swimming pools. You know, it's, it's kind of about changing your mindset in some ways and saying, you know, gosh, I never thought of my business as one to target to the affluent, but maybe sit down and say to yourself instead, here are the reasons why I should target my business there. Yeah, and I think, you know, you mentioned earlier that, you know, a good percentage of these people, from Millionaire Next Door Up, have gotten there through their own devices. I mean, they didn't inherit it and they're not sports stars. No. With a lucky arm. I mean, for the most part, they got there by building businesses and professions. And so they have a mindset about this. First of all, they're not easily frightened. And I find that they're even sort of proudly defiant when it comes to economic downturn. As Phil Graham said the other day, certainly unwisely, politically, but perhaps accurately, about the nation of winers. You know, that, that they, that they resent that don't want to be a part of it and sort of take some joy and pleasure and satisfaction in going ahead and buying a pool when no one else is buying bulls. And a bigger and better pool. Yeah, and a bigger and better pool. So your sense of this and you study this is that, you know, I call them least and last affected by recession. Your sense of this is that now in near future, you don't see a big pullback on their part. No, the people that have pulled back are the people that I'll call the marginal, marginally wealthy. And when times are good, they're wealthy. But when times are bad, they're not because their stock portfolios have been hit their house values been hit. So they're not wealthy anymore. Those aren't the people I'm talking about. I'm talking about the people that are, you know, they, they're fine, regardless of whether the stock market gyrates by, you know, 15% or, you know, and, and their house, you know, these aren't people that were running around buying their houses in the bubble. You know, I mean, the, the one thing I can tell you about this group of people is they are so in tune with finance, the finance world. And I'm going to use one good example. One morning I was just making follow up calls and every single person I was getting sick and you call me back in two hours and I'm thinking, what is this two hour thing? Why did I get this guy to go, you know, what the Fed chairman's speaking right now? Can you call me back in two hours? And I realized all these people were watching the Fed chairman. Yeah, a normal consumer, they would tell you to call back in two hours because they're watching American Idol. Right. These guys were all yesterday sitting on the edge of their seats from a green stand, I had to say. But, but just a silly story like that helps you really understand that, that yes, they were in a recession and we all know that, but are they affected by it? And have they pulled their heads into a shell? No, not at all. Well, it is the story also makes a point that, that I emphasize with people is that, and I find a lot of people just do not do it. If you do want to be effective at marketing and selling to a particular group, really whatever that group is, whether we're talking about an occupational group, chiropractors, dentists, lawn and garden people, restaurant owners, we're talking about the millionaire next door, we're talking about the ultra fluent, you really need to read, to be where they are. So you need to kind of get a grip on what they read and who they pay attention to and where they go and what their water cooler and cocktail party conversation is. And start reading those same things and watching those same things and being able to have those conversations, even if you are not yet in that same economic league, which would you agree? Yeah, I mean, and again, you know, the Wall Street Journal, I never walked in one CEO's office that didn't have the Wall Street Journal sitting there. They all read the Wall Street Journal. And I'm not saying everybody has to go advertise in the Wall Street Journal. No, but read the Wall Street Journal. I mean, they're very, they're in tune with the financial markets. This group of people is very politically active, regardless of which side of the spectrum they fall on. They're very involved in politics and they're very involved in causes. And that's why I mentioned the charity thing before. All the wealthy people I knew, I mean, some of them had their own pet charities, you know, that they just started themselves. Because it was a passion, you know, of, you know, something, you know, and, you know, once they became wealthy, they just started this charity. So if you have a particular individual that you want to target, go research him. You know, go find out all about, you know, whoever it might be and you're going to find a charity somewhere. I'm amazed that the salespeople who don't do research on individual prospects that they are going to make presentations to or that are within a group that they are target marketing, especially since it's gotten so easy to do it. I mean, it used to be you had to go to the library. Right. I mean, now you can just type somebody's name into Google and you, you know, you should be able to find out something about them. Let me get to one last topic before we give people an opportunity to get at you with their specific questions and their own businesses. Let's talk a little bit about referrals. How this group or groups differ from others in the willingness to provide them, whether they can be incentivized. And of course, it's my belief that they are, if anything, more multiple because the more fluent you are, the more you tend to look for peer recommendations. And since they're more valuable, one referral from one of these is like 10 referrals from everybody else. Exactly. What's your experience with the whole referral issue? Well, I mean, I can tell you by the time I was in my territory about 18 months, I got 50% of my business from referrals. And how I kind of built up this network, I mean, and I, like when I first took over my sales territory, I contacted all, I mean, I only had like 60, 50 or 60 owners, you know, I wasn't dealing with a huge customer base. You know, so I contacted all of them individually and I sent them a letter and made my introduction and I tried to talk to all of them personally. And what I can tell you is, you know, the people that I found who ended up being my best referral sources were the people who I solved problems for. So for instance, you know, one particular gentleman, you know, something was always wrong in his catering order, you know, for all the catering that goes on the plane. Well, I got that fixed for him. And within like a month, he gave me a referral. It's, I guess you would just call it the law of reciprocation. You know, once some people felt that I had done something for them, they were then willing to give me a referral. The other thing that I would tell you is, if you're, you know, saying to yourself, well, gee, how would I develop a referral program? You know, maybe I already have, you know, base of affluent customers, you know, and I'd like to get more referrals, but I don't really know how or what kind of program to develop. You know, what I always say is, go talk to your owners, go talk to your clients and just say to them, you know, we're thinking of putting together a kind of a thank you referral program for our customers. We're thinking of putting together a kind of a thank you referral program for our clients who refer other people. If we were to do something like that, what might be something you'd like to get? Because too many companies, you know, develop a referral program based on what they want to give. And what they want to give is not necessarily what the client wants to get. They're just getting the incentive that, you know, the clients actually say, well, gee, if you were to do this, these would be the kinds of things I'd like to have. By the way, the great marketing formula in general is, is the template is, if we were to do X, what would you like us to put in X? And whether that's creating a seminar or creating a referral program or creating a new service, and then you get to go back to everybody and say, here, we built what you wanted. Now you're sort of obligated to participate in it. Exactly. Yeah, it's a great template. And the thing that you'll find is, you know, I mean, and I would always have people, you know, come up with, you know, well, this wasn't on your list and I'd really like to have this. I mean, fine, let me go run it by management and see what they say. And they always said yes, because, I mean, and this is an outrageous number to most people, but when I started selling jets in 1999, every owner that we got cost us $75,000. So if I, you know, if I got it, you know, once you got your referral engine going, that $75,000 came down an awful lot. So if we gave an owner something worth $15,000 for the referral, that was still a big win-win. I've always said, and I talk with people about this in terms of price all the time, I'd rather be selling at premium prices and premium margins. I can afford the highest cost per sale, not trying to have the lowest cost per sale. And then within that framework, to stimulate and reward referrals, you can also spend a lot of money. Exactly. You can do something good. And we found that, you know, the owners, I mean, again, I got so much in my business through referrals that, you know, I didn't have to do a lot of my own marketing in my territory. You know, the company was doing, you know, their own, you know, the marketing on a national basis. But, you know, the referrals do two things. First of all, you know your owner is pretty satisfied with your product if he's giving you a referral, because nobody is referring people when they're unhappy. So if you've got owners giving referrals, you know that they're satisfied customers. And secondly, the person who is the referee, we'll call them, they've already gotten that peer recommendation based on, you know, their friend Joe at the Country Club saying, why don't you call Susan Adams? I mean, Ron Howard called me one day, the producer. You know, Opie, the... Yeah, we just... He was his neighbor in Greenwich, Connecticut, gave him my card. And he just called me up one day. Yeah, and by the way, you would...not that you couldn't, but it would not be easy to get to him cold. I...yeah, I mean, we could certainly have found him via zip codes. Yep. But, you know, I mean, he was just, you know, at his neighbor's house, they were talking about jets, and this guy said, well, I belong to this program. Let me get my rep's name, give her a call. She'll help you out. All right, I have an eye on the clock. We promised people question time. But before we head in that direction, I do want to give you an opportunity to explain the resources you have available. You have a couple of excellent ways to be of help to our people who are in the process of figuring all this out. So let's have the commercial. Well, I'll make it quick and painless. The Selling to Millionaires program is something that... Like all the things I've talked about tonight, it's all these types of things. And what I've done with the program is I've developed workbooks that actually help you bring it to your business. I mean, because I know, as I sit here and talk about jets and things, you know, it sounds nice, but you say to yourself, well, how do I do this in my world? And that's what it's all about, is your world. And that's what this program does, is it takes my experiences and what I know, and it brings it down to a workable level for you in your particular business, in your particular area. That's our little commercial interruption. And that's it? That's it. That's all you want, huh? Because you're... Okay. All right. We'll remind everybody of website to email address again once more before we get done here. I suppose one of the things I didn't ask you and people are always fascinated, a good celebrity story? And then we'll do a Q&A. Well, the Ron Howard one is always kind of funny because he called me at like 7.30 in the morning, which I was in my office because I always get in early. And I was sitting here in like sweatpants and a sweatshirt. It was the middle of the winter and I just, you know, I was sitting in this and I had like fuzzy bunny slippers on. And there I was talking to Ron Howard. Well, that's very Andy Griffithy. Yes. You know, the interesting thing is, is that most celebrities could not afford fractional ownership. They actually get private air travel through a lot of the movie studios. But we didn't actually have a lot of celebrities. We had mostly business owners, golfers. We had a lot of golfers and athletes, but not... We didn't have the Brad and the Angelinas and people like that. Now, although when you sell your baby pictures for 11 million... Once again, ladies and gentlemen, if you have a question, please press star zero now and an operator will take your name. Let me know when you're ready. Okay, thanks, Candice. So it's www.infusionsoft.com forward slash Dan Kennedy Tour. And also, I do want to encourage you to get over to Susan's site. And Susan, do you want to give that website once more? Sure. It's www.sellingtomillionaireswithan-s.net. Okay. Candice, I think we're ready. Okay. Our first question comes from Nancy. Nancy, please state your question. Hi. What about in the service industry? Would you approach it any differently? And where would you begin on a local basis? Well, you know, interestingly enough, Nancy, I mean, our business was a service business. I mean, even though we were selling airplanes, we actually ran a fleet of 100 airplanes. But at the end of the day, it was a service business. And it was a service business because you could go out and buy the best and biggest of what we had. We had to deliver it on the service end. You know, the planes had to show up on time. The catering had to be there. The pilots had to be friendly. So service is, especially with the affluent, they respond to a service message. Because at the end of the day, even if you're a car dealer, they want to look at you and say, well, how are you going to take care of me as a customer? And that, you know, is, again, the service message really applies to any business. And it is a very big issue of how am I going to be cared for after I give you money? I kind of know what it's going to be like before I give you money. There's a particular cruise line that I will never cruise on again, because everything was great until you were disembarking. And now you had already spent, they had already gotten every penny they could get. And we were on concierge level. You couldn't spend any more money than I had spent to take this cruise. And they had already sold you artwork, photographs, jewelry, rock massages, you know, everything else they could sell to you. And they had stolen your money in the casino. And they had done it with elegance and grace. And everything was great until leaving the ship. And that was ugly. And then there was a problem. And the follow-up on the problem was horrendous. And so, you know, we've all had this experience of being romanced well and then taking care of badly. And I think that's where your promises, your guarantees, your unique selling propositions really have power and impact with the affluent client. What kind of service business is Nancy? Well, he's, and I've been on other calls before and talked about work, but my husband specifically is a chiropractor, but he doesn't do chiropractic. He's mastered nutrition. He changes people's lives around and helps them lose weight and just does wonderful things for them. And he's said a couple times before, you know, I should just take a couple of patients a month and be with them one-on-one and charge them an arm and a leg, and I could turn their lives around. So where would you begin with that? Well, he's about the arm and a leg analogy, especially given that he is a client. Might not be the right analogy. But whatever, that sounds kind of painful. But I mean, look, he's got it, you know, close the door, make it a leap, make it exclusive, kind of live with him and charge him a big fee. Susan, what other advice would you ask for? Well, I mean, you can create exclusivity in different ways. And again, I remember on your call last week, someone asked about it. And scarcity is the best way to create exclusivity. It's the riskiest because you're saying, okay, we're only going to take this number. But everybody wants what they can't have. And when you start finding, you know, when you say, okay, well, we're only going to take seven new patients or, you know, new clients, then the first seven, once they're in, the door is shut. There's no more. I mean, and I can tell you, you know, just, you know, again, back to these people that would be worth millions and millions of dollars. You know, a friend of mine who sells Gulf Streams, they released, they introduced a new plane about three months ago, and it's like a $50 million plane. There's already a huge waiting list for it. And it won't even be out for four years. You know, Ferrari does this every year. I think they produce maybe 500 cars a year, and they won't produce any more than that. They've created their own backlog and their own demands simply by not producing that many. Well, the other thing to get to is that in your, in this particular category, you not only have exclusivity and personal attention to sell, you have privacy and discretion to sell. I mean, the person with a net worth of above a million, they may do it, but they're out of dire necessity and no other options, but they are not thrilled about marching over to Jenny Craig. Yeah. So, you know, they would be taking their taxes to each and our block. You know, that's just not an acceptable environment for them. And so you really, by making this service private, have an opportunity to be charging for that privacy. And with that, we're going to have to cut you off. And Candice, let's get the next person in. The next question comes from Eric. Eric, please state your question. Hello, Dan. Hello, Susan. Hello. My question is if we sell to the affluent business owners, should we start with from an introductory product or should we just pitch the high value product directly? Well, what kind of product are you selling or talking about? We might be selling, let's say, customer software solutions. Should we start from some maybe lower-take-it-value product first and should we just pitch them the high end? Well, I think you have to think about your product in the terms, again, in terms of value. You know, which product brings more value to that affluent customer? And how are you going to relate that value to the customer? So, you know, what I always encourage my clients to do is, you know, get inside the head of the buyer and say, okay, which product would I want and why would I want it? And I think if you're able to answer that question, you're able to say, this is the product we should approach them with. You know, whichever one creates more value for them and how it creates that value. Yeah, I think there's two things. One is that, again, it's very hard to shake yourself loose from being all about the product. And so thinking about the marketing and the sales process has one of sort of pushing product. And then framing the question is, which product should we push first? As opposed to making it all about the buyer, who they are, really what they are looking for most in need of, will value most, how they determine value, so that you let that determine everything, including product choices, price choices, marketing processes, and so forth. The other thing is I would refer back to an earlier part of our conversation. The one thing about low price entry points versus high price products, especially in B2B, where you may endure longer sales cycles, more complex sales processes, you have get past gatekeepers, earn sales opportunity with decision maker issues, is more often than not, you're going to be better served being able to incur a high cost per sale than trying to deal with low cost per sale. And just answering it blind without asking you 50 smart questions, which Susan would do if she coached you privately. But my off the cuff answer blind is, more often than not, I'd rather be playing the game able to spend a lot of money to make that sale than able to spend a little. And we hope that helps, right? Okay, next question comes from Matt. Matt, please state your question. Hello, I'm a physical therapist and one of 125 people in the world trained with an advanced level of dealing with the nervous system. I'm looking to build a concierge medical practice and just curious as to some suggestions as to how to get started before my budget is large enough to be throwing money at the clients like I might. Okay. Susan, you can go first. Well, I mean, I think I would recommend, you know, some of the things we've already talked about on the call tonight, which would be getting out amongst that community and networking with them. And you can certainly, you know, as you look at your local community and you look at some of the, you know, the charity events that are going on and, you know, ways and opportunities for you to network, you know, either through other business owners that you know, and really just start getting your message out there for, you know, this is who we are. This is why we're different. This is what makes us unique. This is, you know, this is what would attract an affluent buyer or client to my service. Now make sure that you have that message down. You have your, you know, your reason for this is why people are, you know, coming to us and you have your good message down before you actually go out and start networking and start meeting these people. Because if, I mean, the worst thing that I've ever seen people do is they kind of put themselves in the position before they're ready with a message and you need to have your message ready before you go out and deliver that message. And you can use it. I think that's, you know, sage advice. I mean, look, it's not necessarily about having to throw money at it. Often if you don't have budget, you have time and can do a lot of personal networking and personal selling to get the ball rolling. I mean, most concierge practices are conversions where a lot of it is simply taking chairs away. There's 2000 patients in the practice now and we announced we're going to change the rules of the game and only accept 200. And the other 1800 are going to be orphaned and have to go elsewhere. The building of a concierge type practice from scratch is a very different proposition. But the most important thing will be honing that message, which you have a sentence start to it. I want to have only 125 in the world. But honing that message down so that when you do have the opportunity to present it to affluent individuals, it's absolutely right. It's extremely important. But the other call or two, Nancy, I mean, this is the future of healthcare for the affluent, is private healthcare. And so, you know, the trend favors you at the moment. I mean, these are the people that you read about that just go get these checkups, you know, where they spend a day and get full body scans and MRIs. Yeah, they'll spend like $5,000 just getting a health checkup and none of it's covered through insurance. No, see, well, that's right. That's back to the price is not the issue, right? Exactly. I mean, they just they want to assure themselves that, you know, that they look clean for cancer or whatever it might be. Yeah, and there's definitely a segment of the affluent market that is very alternative health oriented and very anti traditional medicine that you can appeal to. Watching the clock. Candice, let's let another person have a turn. We didn't we didn't lose Candice there, did we? Yeah, I'm still right here. Okay. Next question comes from Dwight Dwight, please state your question. Hi, Dan. Hi, Susan. Hey Dwight. Well, let me tell you this short story. Guy comes in to buy a kickboxing membership for himself and his and his new wife. And it's just 400 bucks. He pulls out the black American Express card, which I had always heard of, but I'm not going to pretend I've never seen one myself. So but he hasn't started and I'm just wondering in my communications with him, do I have to do anything different than my normal communications? Well, it's a little late, but you could have added a zero. That's the thing. The second he pulled it out, Dan, I thought of you and I thought, Dan, I should have asked for more. Yeah, you can't change your price now. I was just wondering, you know, and follow up calls to him or what have you, even though I just was told that he started tonight, so maybe it wouldn't be necessary. Well, so so let's just frame it so you discover maybe after a sale has even taken place that you have an especially affluent customer or client. That way. And Susan, is there anything now that you do that's smart that you wouldn't have done otherwise? Well, I think what I wouldn't do is give him the impression that you're glomming on. Right. Yeah, that just doesn't work like that. There's nothing that wealthy people hate more than someone, you know, that suddenly they pull out the black card and now you're like, you know, shimmying over like, oh, let me get to know you. No, I mean, I mean, obviously he's someone who can certainly give you referrals of other, you know, friends of his. You know, but I would just I would follow up in just a normal friendly manner of, you know, is there a way we can improve things for you? How do you like the course? You know, how would you like it to be different? Is there any, you know, this is just and make it in the context of this is just a normal client survey. Right. You know, don't give him the impression that, you know, we're doing this just for you because again, I saw you pull out that black card. Just, you know, be very friendly and say, you know, we always survey our clients a weekend to, you know, two weeks in just to see how the experience is going. If there's anything you'd like to be different and I would approach it that way. You know, that since since reading the book, I've started, you know, just casually taking a look at the automobiles that drive up to my school. And I'm presently surprised and happy to report that it's there's a major amount of Mercedes and BMWs and some Porsches and what have you. Well, yeah, that a by the way, it's amazing how many people don't even think to go do that. But it suggests that a you're doing a lot of right things now that are causing you to appeal to mass affluent it up. And it suggests that you need to, you know, be thinking about is there a higher level of what we offer that can be created so that there's, you know, another price tier on the menu for those who want it. Yeah, it's along the lines of what Nancy just said about her chiropractor husband, because again, because of you and the attention that I've paid to them in my newsletters. A lot of people have approached me and said, you know, you really should get into life coaching. And now I'm starting to think, well, maybe I should have a private program that you still continue your martial art training. But now you have access to me and my resources in terms of life skills and maybe even business skills. Does that sound like? Look, I've a friend in your exact business who got to that exact point accidentally rather than purposefully. Literally, several students literally, you know, sat him down at his desk and said, we want you to do this, and we want to pay you to do it. We want private time. And we want to, you know, we want to keep continuing in martial arts, but we want, you know, to pick your business brain. And we want to think about other issues together. And I remember he got me on the phone and said, these three guys want to give me money, you know, what do I charge them? What the heck do I do? And, you know, that starting point was, you know, them all getting together twice a month and they paid him three grand a month apiece. So he instantly had a $9,000 a month, you know, little coaching group. Right. And so, you know, your clientele will tell you and even tell you further if you go ask them. See, so this comes back to the little template that Susan gave earlier in the call that actually you've seen us use of, okay, if we were going to do X and you were going to participate, what would you like to see X be? And your own clients will help you define that for you. But I'm quite certain there's an opportunity there. Well, thanks for writing the book. I mean, this one has given me more to think about in terms of restructuring than any of the others. Well, that's good. I appreciate it. And don't miss the value for you in the Selling to the Millionaires program either. I won't. Okay. Candice, we can take one more. Okay. Our final question comes from Seth Green. Seth, please state your question. Hi. Thank you so much for the call in the book. Love it as always. As Dan knows, I run a private family wealth management office catering to the affluent and ultra affluent. I'm just wondering what you found, Susan, to be, let's say, the biggest bait in getting people to, as Dan says, raise their hand and request more information. Well, see, for us, it was a little bit different. I mean, we really just did a lot of advertising in the Wall Street Journal and people would call us or we would get referrals. You know, if you're in the, I mean, and I worked a lot with the private wealth guys at like Goldman Sachs and Merrill Lynch, whatever, because a lot of our customers were the same people. Right. And I mean, UBS has a deal with net jets where you get, you know, if you spend, if you have X amount, you get time on a jet. Right. And so, you know, what I would encourage you to do is, you know, are you looking for things in your local market? You know, what specifically are you looking for some guidance with? I mean, because I wasn't in the situation where I had to go out and do a lot of prospecting. Right. I would say at the moment, yes, I mean, we're in Buffalo, New York, and yes, we're expanding in our local market, but from reading the book, I realized that the, as Dan wrote, that the barriers were actually in my head and that we need to, I was at one point, I was thinking that in a few years, I would have an area exclusive program like Dan does with the IVAs and say, okay, Silver Spoon is going national. I'm thinking I may need to do that sooner rather than later because it's a boundary in my head as opposed to physical. And then obviously, when our markets opened up like New York and LA and Chicago, there are a lot more affluent, ultra affluent people there than there are here in Buffalo for sure. Well, I mean, I can tell you, because I have read a lot about, you know, the affluent and financial services, and I've read a lot, you know, Merrill Lynch does a lot of reports with a company called Cap Gemini where they do all these wealth reports. And there's something that they really keyed in on is that what the wealthy are looking for in a financial advisor is, you know, it has to be someone that they can develop trust with, trust and credibility. And I can tell you that, you know, being a salesperson myself, the best way that you can establish trust and credibility with a client is to just simply listen to them. And I know that it's such an elemental sales thing. And when I, you know, I coach people on this, they kind of just look at me like, that's it. It's as simple. I mean, I did most of my selling over the phone. So that is, you know, that's the hardest way to sell because you have no personal interaction. I would simply ask people questions and I would shut up and listen. And I would let them tell me everything they wanted to tell me. And that is what starts to build credibility in the eyes of the client because you are someone who is actually trying to understand their situation. You're not talking about your product. You're not talking about your service. You're not talking about anything but saying to them, Mr. Jens, tell me why you called today. You know, the family office proposition, I'm familiar with it. I've answered, I'm sure you've seen the lead generation third of a page verticals in forums, Wall Street Journal and so forth by the one big company and they give away a free book. The books, the ads great, the books horrid, by the way. But which is actually good news for you and their follow-ups even worse. But the family office proposition is very much like another thing I've been playing prospect with lately is the version of that for healthcare, companies like Pinnacle who you pay a large fee to and an annual fee and they basically are your healthcare manager. And you know, they find you the best doctor for X, Y or Z if you need it and they have preferred access so they can hurry up and get you an earlier appointment at the Cleveland Clinic if you need and so forth. And their rep has been very good about this question thing. Really figuring out what it is that we place value on and are looking for. And then turning around and selling it to us. I mean I think you can get hands raised sort of the usual way with family office proposition being the relief of the mixed bag of pieces with financial advisor over here, stock broker over there, store full of this, store full of that, chaos and confusion. Right, I mean you're the reason that we exist and that we started. We've talked before and you've helped design our marketing materials. I mean we have a CPA on staff to do their taxes. We have lawyers on staff to do their legal documents. We have, I mean you name it that you wrote about, we did. But now Susan's point is, and I had a whole list of things. It's one of the things I didn't get through tonight with her. Susan's point is very well drawn and that is, again, you just described sort of features of the service, right? Yeah. What you really are selling is trust. And in fact I have a saying, I say that be affluent by the person before they buy the product. They absolutely do. And so what you're really selling here is trust. And so you have to construct a process that accelerates the establishment of trust in part and parcel of what you do. And so you have to think about where you generate leads from. And once they do raise their hand, how that process is engineered to create trust. And part of that process is not going to be able to be done through media and distance. It is going to have to be done through conversational selling in order to have them tell you what they want and then turn around and have built it for them. And I hope that is helpful, but we are 30 seconds away from being out of clock. And I don't know if Susan has an opera or a gala to go to Pittsburgh tonight or not, but I do actually have a... No, I actually go walk the dog. There you go. Well, I walked a million dollar dog before this call, so I don't have to say that, but I do have something that I need to do. So I want to thank everybody for being on the call. Remind you about my tour information at infusionsoft.com forward slash Dan Kennedy Tour. Okay, Susan, thank you very much for your time, an excellent call, and again, thank everybody else for tent-tenting. Good night. Thank you.
